turning_points.name: The Bishnoi Collapse
turning_points.description: Bishnoi took three wickets in six balls — Phillips (googly), Sundar (legbreak), and then Tewatia. GT went from 64% favourites chasing smoothly to suddenly needing 78 off 7 overs with half their batting gone. Win probability for GT dropped from 0.64 to 0.35 in under two overs.

turning_points.name: The Rabada-Rashid Revival
turning_points.description: When Shahrukh Khan was run out at 161/7, GT needed 50 off 30 with three wickets in hand. Rabada and Rashid put together an extraordinary partnership — 43 runs at SR 268 — that swung the probability back to 81% in GT's favour. The match's biggest swing occurred here, with the Rabada-Rashid stand moving win probability from 20% to 81%.

turning_points.name: Archer and Deshpande Shut the Door
turning_points.score_context: GT needed 15 off 12 (81.29% favourites) → scored 9, lost by 6
turning_points.description: Archer's 19th over conceded only 5 runs — short balls into Rabada's body, yorkers to Rashid's toes. With 10 off the last over, Deshpande bowled four consecutive yorkers, then had Rashid sliced to Archer at deep point. GT scored 3. The final swing: from 81% to 0% in 12 balls.

counterfactual_scenarios.title: What if Bishnoi Had Been Average?
counterfactual_scenarios.change: Bishnoi bowls 4 overs at 9 runs/over (36 runs) with 0 wickets (vs actual 4/41). Phillips, Sundar, Tewatia survive.
counterfactual_scenarios.counterfactual_outcome: GT would have scored ~224, winning by ~13 runs
counterfactual_scenarios.narrative:
When Bishnoi picked up Sudharsan at 10.4, Gujarat Titans were coasting at 107 for 2, Jos Buttler starting to look genuinely dangerous. In the real match, Bishnoi then removed Phillips at 12.1 and Sundar at 12.3 — two balls, two wickets, two left-handers who should theoretically have the advantage against a legspinner but ran into the one bowler who tilts that equation. If those two had survived — if Bishnoi's googly to Phillips had turned six inches less, if his legbreak to Sundar had missed the midwicket sweeper — GT would have reached the 13th over with 131 for 2 and two fresh batsmen at the crease. Buttler could have pushed on instead of trying to compensate for a collapsing batting order.

The counterfactual arithmetic is brutal in its clarity. With Phillips and Sundar intact, GT's batting lineup retained depth all the way to Rashid and Rabada. The catastrophic middle-order collapse that shrank GT's target from eminently manageable to desperately difficult simply doesn't happen. Instead of Rashid and Rabada needing 30 off 3 overs, they enter the final phase as the team's 9th and 10th wickets in a comfortable chase. The difference between 4/41 and 0/36 across four overs is not just runs and wickets — it is the entire architecture of the match.

Bishnoi takes the ball in the 11th over, GT 64% favourites with nine wickets remaining, and unravels it all in 18 deliveries. That's the leverage point. Without it, this is a comfortable Gujarat Titans victory in a match nobody remembers.

counterfactual_scenarios.title: What if Rabada Had Connected on the Full Toss?
counterfactual_scenarios.change: In Archer's 19th over, Rabada makes contact with the final-ball full toss that he missed in reality. The ball travels for 4 runs. GT need 11 off the last over (not 10)... wait, or actually: if it went for 6, GT need 9 off last over.
counterfactual_scenarios.original_outcome: GT needed 10 off last over, scored 3, lost by 6 runs
counterfactual_scenarios.counterfactual_outcome: If full toss went for 6: GT needed 4 off last over — highly winnable
counterfactual_scenarios.narrative:
There is a moment in Archer's 19th over — the match effectively decided but the probability still hovering — when Kagiso Rabada faces a full toss from Jofra Archer and misses it completely. Not a good ball. A gift. The match report notes it plainly: 'That could've changed the game.' In the real world, Rabada missed, the ball lobbed to the keeper, and GT needed 10 off the final Deshpande over. They scored 3.

In the counterfactual, Rabada makes contact. A full toss from Archer — bowled in an effort to reverse-swing it or deceive with change of pace — is pure batting territory. If it flies for six rather than being missed entirely, GT's equation transforms from 'desperately difficult' to 'genuinely possible' in 0.3 seconds. They need 4 off Deshpande's final over: a single boundary, or any combination of boundaries, gets them home. Rashid Khan at the non-striker end, still fresh, waiting.

Deshpande is extraordinary under pressure — he had bowled four consecutive yorkers in his penultimate over — but defending 4 runs off 6 balls with Rashid Khan able to get on strike is a very different proposition from defending 10. The probability flips from 19% to something north of 70%. The margin between winning and losing this match is not six runs across twenty overs. It is one missed full toss in the 19th.

commentary_gems.text: sliced out to the fence, and Archer snares the chance! Deshpande is having the night of his life under the Ahmedabad lights! On a length and hung up wide outside off. Rashid throws the plumbing at it but he slices it way too much.

off stump thumped, and Rabada has the last laugh! On a length and zoning into off stump. Jaiswal backs away, with designs to go inside-out. Comfortably late on the stroke, though, and the ball whistles past the bat before leaving off stump in a mess.

shanked and taken! Big, big moment in the game! Not the best ball Bishnoi will ever bowl but he will take it. Of course he will. A bit of a drag-down on middle and leg. Sudharsan shapes up for the mighty swipe over deep mid wicket but skews it straight down Deshpande's throat.
commentary_gems.context: Bishnoi removes Sudharsan — the turning point

commentary_gems.text: banged in much shorter outside off, and Jurel nicks it through! Looks for the same stroke, trying to upper-cut it over the bowler. This has just enough kick on it to make the shot untenable. A brilliant knock but someone else will need to take RR close to 210 now!

game_changers.stat: 4/41 in 4 overs — the match's decisive bowling performance
game_changers.reason: With GT cruising at 127/2 in the 12th over, 64% likely to win the game, Bishnoi dismantled the chase in 18 deliveries. He dismissed Sudharsan, Phillips, Sundar, and Tewatia — each to different variations, each timed perfectly. The googly that beat Sudharsan, the legbreak that Sundar swept to midwicket: Bishnoi bowls with a clarity of purpose that is rare in T20 cricket. He didn't just take wickets; he reset the entire probability architecture of the match.

game_changers.stat: 75(42): 5×4, 5×6, SR 178.57 — the innings that set the target
game_changers.reason: Jurel's 75 was not just runs accumulated — it was strokeplay that made the opposition's best plans look redundant. He hit a Rashid wicket-to-wicket skidder to the boundary between midwicket and long-on with a straight bat; he charged the same bowler and went over long-on for six. After Jaiswal's dismissal at 126/2, Jurel needed to bat 17 overs of escalating pressure alone. His partnership with Jadeja (39 off ~4 overs) gave RR the buffer that held.

In RR's innings, Rabada was GT's most consistent bowler — dismissing both Jaiswal and Ferreira while conceding only 10.5 RPO against a batting order in full flight. In the chase, when GT were 161/7 and the match looked done, Rabada produced batting of real quality — 23 off 16 with two sixes — bringing GT to the brink of a stunning victory. He was inches away from being the match-winner. On another night, he is.

game_changers.stat: 73(44): 9×4, 3×6, SR 165.90 — the innings that almost won it
game_changers.reason: Sudharsan reached his fifty in 33 balls — the same number it took Jaiswal, the man with the dasher's reputation. He ramped Archer for six in the first over. He kept pace with the required rate through the powerplay and into the middle overs when others fell around him. When Bishnoi finally dismissed him at 107/2, GT had 64% of the match won. The collapse that followed was not because of Sudharsan; it was because of everything Bishnoi could do that others couldn't.
